affected_groups: students affected by segregation via multiple channels
teachers and administrators tasked with implementation
content.decision_question: Should reform proceed as a coordinated bundle addressing tracking age, admissions, zoning, and legal exemptions simultaneously, accepting higher implementation complexity, or should it proceed lever-by-lever to preserve feasibility at the cost of leaving some segregation channels unaddressed for longer?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence on implementation capacity, workforce constraints, and historical precedent for single- versus multi-lever reforms can clarify feasibility trade-offs and likely sequencing risks, but it cannot determine how much risk of partial failure is acceptable in exchange for comprehensiveness — that prioritization between ambition and deliverability is a policy value choice, not something resolved by more data.
content.tension: The Portuguese whole-system lens and the multiple-independent-channels finding (tracking age, admissions design, zoning/choice, legal exemptions) show that no single proposal addresses all segregation drivers, implying coordinated bundled reform (as in T6's linkage to T4) is more likely to succeed; yet pursuing multiple simultaneous levers multiplies political, legal, and administrative-capacity demands within a system already constrained by an aging teacher workforce and KLIK centralization, raising a genuine trade-off between comprehensiveness and deliverability.
content.title: Single-lever versus coordinated multi-channel reform given resource and political-capital constraints
value_poles: comprehensiveness and effectiveness of coordinated multi-channel reform
feasibility and manageable implementation burden given constrained capacity
